sentences.text: No, I was not making preparations.
I was just down on a-- the decision was made on the spot.
I still did not believe that the Germans would break so quickly through.
And I thought that Warsaw, at least temporary, would be safe.
We were told the Polish army is strong, and God knows what is it.
As we found it out, it was-- it collapsed.
This was a-- look, this was on the 28th, I think, of August.
This was when the mobilization was announced in Poland.
And the war broke out on the 1st of September.
So it was four days before the outbreak of the war.
The mobilization was very badly arranged.
[NON-ENGLISH] was not prepared-- they were unable able to mobilize, were changing the spots where people should go, and nothing was well organized.
The war broke out [SIGHS] in the early morning hours of the 1st.
We heard the first bombardments.
I was in Warsaw by then, too.
But I was staying with my girlfriend, whereby my parents were staying in another part of town.
I had connections, I knew people, and I found out-- on the 4th, I think--in the morning, of September, that the Polish government is evacuating.
And the Germans broke through from the north.
So I decided to leave Warsaw.
You couldn't-- there was no petrol, you couldn't have cars.
You couldn't-- I didn't have my car then with me.
So I decided to walk from Warsaw, and I went out with a cousin of my girlfriend, who was a lawyer, too.
Older than I was.
And we walked not very far, because then we got a train, and then we rented a-- from a Polish peasant, a horse and a buggy-- how do you say?
He took us on it a while.
And then on the way to Lublin, at what moment passed us a group of trucks.
And one of the trucks belonged to a sugar mill in which my-- which was owned by the bank and by the family of my girlfriend-- and the family of the cousin of hers.
And we got on the-- on them-- and with this-- on this truck.
We came to Lublin, which was a town in Poland, a comparatively large town southwest from Warsaw.
And as I was sitting in the coffee house the next morning in Lublin, on the street, I saw a number of cars going, one after the other.
They were going slow, because plenty of refugees, and the roads were blocked, and so on.
And I noticed that this was my uncle's car, and my car.
And so I started-- I remember, I pushed the little table over and started running behind them.
And I picked up the motorcade, and it was my uncle going with the diplomatic corps.
He was going, actually, with Mr.
Trianov who was a Bulgarian ambassador in.
Poland And I joined them.
So I was going for a while with the diplomatic corps.
Then I saw the disorder, how little and stupid the members of this diplomatic corps were.
How they were subject to all the alarming news and gossip and what have you.
Among others, there was Biddle, who was the American ambassador from the very well known Boston family.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Somewhere on the way, and I don't remember where, my uncle noticed the British consul in Katowice, who he knew, Mr.
Twades.
And he started talking with Twades, and Twades told him that if we joined him, he would arrange for us-- he would come over to Romania with us to Palestine.
And we started going with them.
And here-- she was always asking if she can drive the car.
So she was driving the car.
In the long run, we came to Siemianowice, which already was south of Poland, not very far from the Romanian border.
As we arrived in Siemianowice, the British ambassador was already there.
And she asked if she could-- she had some luggage in our car-- if she can take the car and stay with the Ambassador.
We couldn't stay in the same house, we found us some other accommodations.
And I told my uncle, not to-- I said, don't trust her, not to give her a car.
And my uncle told me, said, I'm stupid.
Maybe he was right.
[LAUGHS] Anyway, so he let her take the car, but he didn't have peace.
All right, so we found ourself in Siemianowice without any possibility to go further.
We met in Siemianowice with two lawyers, Poles from Katowice.
And they had a car, a little Citroen and-- no, no, no.
And we got into this car with them, and we decided not to go to the Romanian border, because we didn't trust the Romanians.
But to go back, and see eventually to go to Wilno.
We came as far as Dubno-- it was all in the-- and over there, we met with Mrs.
Korfanty.
Korfanty was one of the top politicians in Poland, out of the National Party, or Christian National Party.
He died.
This was widow of Korfanty.
And as we are standing with her, came to us a guy in civilian clothes with a major.
And he approached Mrs.
Korfanty, because he knew her.
And who it was-- it was General Sikorski, who later became the head of the Polish government in exile.
And who later was bumped up by Churchill, I think.
Because Churchill gave him plenty of undertakings, and he didn't know how to get out of it.
And he had some documents.
And he said that he will try-- illegally-- to get through to France, to Hungary and France.
So it wsa the last I I saw of him.
He succeeded-- got to Paris and became the head of the Polish Committee, and then Polish government in exile.
We stayed and moved further north, going towards Wilno.
And two days later, the Russians came and took over the eastern part of Poland.
Obviously, they came to Sarny, this was the place that we were.
They took the car, what have you.
For the first time, I saw the russian army.
And from Sarny, they-- few days later by train-- we got to Wilno.
In Wilno one looked at the possibility, eventually, to get to Sweden, and to escape this way.
While I was in Wilno, some disturbing news were coming from Warsaw.
And I did the-- an agreement was made between the Russians and the Lithuanians, that the Lithuanians would take over Wilno.
And I was frightened that I wouldn't get-- that I would be completely blocked-- that the Germans, in due course, would take it over.
So I decided to go south, and I went to Bialystok.
And from Bialystok, I didn't have any news from my parents.
I did a very daring thing-- I went between the Russian and German lines, on the Bug River, to Warsaw.
I was caught by the Russians.
And with the Russians, you never knew what happened.
They caught me at the same time they caught people who came from the other side-- from the western side-- from Warsaw, into the Russian zone, Jewish people.
I got all right into Warsaw, this was 23rd of November, '39.
I was, for three days, in Warsaw with my parents.
When I arrived in Warsaw by night, it was a curfew.
So when I came into the station, there was an information desk.
So I went to him, and the guy said to me, look, if I would be you, there's SS everywhere around-- I wouldn't be staying here.
If you arrive after the curfew by night, by train, I have special passes you can go.
And I went-- the streets were empty, completely.
When I came and knocked at the door, and the caretaker opened the door, and he saw me, he told to them-- couldn't believe that somebody could get through.
I went up to the apartment my parents were in.
And as I came to the door, the dogs smelled me.
And he hit, you know, the door-- started hitting the door.
My parents were sure that the Gestapo is-- and I was dirty.
And I was with my parents.
As a matter of fact, my girlfriend, with her mother, joined them, because their apartment was bumped.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I talk to my parents, I talk to my girlfriend.
I advise them to go, to come with me, to try to get through the lines again.
To come under the Russians, because I considered it safer than under the Germans.
There was no ghetto yet, but-- and no armbands, but it was going very badly.
There were already people arrested, shot, what have you.
I successfully got through the border, found myself back in Bialystok.
From there I got to Lwow, and in Lwow I decided that I will try to go over to Hungary.
My cousin was the head of the medical department of this part of the Polish army, which was close to Hungary.
I had communication here, I met his wife, and they had communication from him.

The arrangement was that if we had some people who are guiding us, that when we reached a particular spot on the Hungarian side, there would be a car which would take us to Mukacevo and then to Budapest.
And I had the address of my cousin in Budapest, who was working-- who was with the Polish embassy over there, as well.
Or living next to the Polish embassy.
Unfortunately, this young man-- it was very strong winter of '39, December.
And everything was freezing in the mountains, and deep snow.
And he couldn't walk, he was falling down in the snow, it was very hard walking.
And we came down on the Hungarian side, where they started already to get-- day was coming.
And the peasants, where we were supposed to go, were frightened to take us in, because they were petrified of the Hungarian patrols.
We were taken to the Hungarian commanding officer of the region, who coincidentally spoke Polish like a Pole.
Must have been a mixed family or something.
There were some Poles brought in, too.
A Polish officer-- army officer and a Polish police officer.
And they were bloody begging the Hungarians to throw us over [LAUGHS] the wall there.
Anyway, the Hungarian officer obliged, and we were taken to a post.
He made an exception for this young boy.
This guy-- doctor, he was a doctor and baptized, you know.
[LAUGHS] Didn't help him.
And I and the doctor-- Ole was his name-- I and him were later, by night, thrown over the border.
So we went a little bit and came back again to go to the next.
I can tell you, it was a very difficult thing.
Because it was very cold, it was freezing.
I had a piece of bread, I had to throw it out because it was completely frozen.
We couldn't use it.
We got out in the next town, and we, again, were taken to the military commander.
Unfortunately, as we were escorted to him, a guy from the army group-- which was in the previous town-- saw us and he denounced us.
From the camp, on the 31st of December-- this was 24th of December, we were arrested-- on the 31st of December, we were loaded into trucks from Germany to Russia.
We didn't know where.
After a few days, I think six or seven days-- very cold conditions, freezing conditions, goods trucks-- we arrived at the station.
And I saw the writing on the station-- [NON-ENGLISH].
It's a Swedish burial place.
And then I knew that the Sweden were-- attacked Russia, and the the Russian-- hit very strongly near Poltava, and over on the Swedes.
Because I knew that we were somewhat close to Poltava.
I knew Poltava-- this is where we're taken to jail.
I was in Poltava jail for a few months.
I was in a small cell with three others, with whom I didn't have any communication.
So I was, more, or less, all the day talking to myself.
[LAUGHS] The Poltava jail was comparatively clean, we are four, only.
Our food was a piece of bread, some tea in the morning, some-- a bit of sugar-- a spoon of sugar-- and some so-called soup in the evening.
I didn't know at this time what the charges-- because I was not put-- at this time-- in front of the court, which I actually never was.
Another matter.
Obviously, the charge was illegal coming through the border.
They started in Poltava, taking us for interviews.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] No, this was not in Poltava, this was later in Kremenchuk.
After staying in Poltava, I don't remember how long, they put us in these Black Maria [LAUGHS] trucks, and into a train again.
They inherited, from the Czar regime, the special railway cars for prisoners.
They were the same cars as what was in Sardy.
[LAUGHS] And, we were transported always inside this, every train had such a car.
And we were transported in this car, we came to Kremenchuk, which is another town on the Ukraine.
And there we found ourselves already in a larger cell with a number of people.
And from there, we were starting being taken for interrogations.
And the system what always won.
You are not permitted to sleep during the day.
During the night, the light was always bright, and we are not permitted to cover ourselves up.
So there's a guy who was looking through the spyhole.
So we are not there.
We were taken and started the investigation.
It was a stupid investigations.
And they were done this way to make you tired.
So they took you, and asked you some stupid questions, and ask you with a couple of guys for a couple of hours.
And then they put you into the out-- and say, go back to your cell.
They let you to fall asleep for half an hour, and they call you out again.
And they found photos of me, so they wanted to know who this person is.
And still, really very same questions.
Said I was a spy, and what have you, and-- Then from jail, one night, I was called out.
We all were called out, one by one, and we were read our sentences.
We would put so-called-- Russian system was called [RUSSIAN]-- special commission of three.
Which supposedly was-- what they were doing, they had a stamp.
One would stamp three years, one stamp five years, one eight, one 10.
And one was-- and certain percentage got three, certain percentage got five, certain eight, certain 10.
I got three.
From there were taken to Kharkiv to a special jail, which was a transport jail, from which they would send later to concentration camp.
Until then, we were kept completely separate from Russians.
In Kharkiv, by mistake, they put two Russians in with us.
And I saw one of them had a face, a very prominent face, of a very intelligent guy.
And in the cell, where we are 200 in a cell, which was a bit bigger than these two rooms.
So we were lying by night later, we had to lie like sardines-- in the nude, one into the other.
Anyway, I moved to this guy somewhere, and I started talking to him.
And this was the most intelligent, the brightest guy I ever met in my life.
Has had a very interesting story, he was early communist.
In the early '20s, he got disillusioned with the communism, and he was arrested.
And with the help of his friends, who were all of high positions from his party, he escaped.
And he was hidden for all the years from '22 or something, until '39-- all over Russia.
You could see he had hands which never worked.
Very interesting story.
Polyglot, speak a number of languages.
And he more or less told me what is going to happen.
He knew everything, and he was a personal friend of Sedov.
Sedov was a son of Trotsky, who was later killed in Paris on Stalin's orders.
He was his personal friend.
He was friendly with all the hierarchy in the communist party.
And he gave me a colossal picture of this, what was happening, and what is going to happen here in Russia.
He did, himself, a stupid thing, that he was amnesty.
Announced that his friends told him that if he goes and makes himself available, they will let him out.
Which was not true.
I don't know what happened to him later.
After a couple of hours, the Russian authorities realized the mistake, and they took those two guys out.
And I never saw them again.
Anyway, from Kharkiv, we were sent-- put into trucks, again.
And we were going for over two weeks.
And we knew that we are going to concentration camp, but I didn't know where.
I wanted to tell you one thing.
The condition in Kharkiv jail, particularly, were terrible.
We were taken twice a day to a toilets.
And these toilets we were going through dirt up to here.
You know, it was unbelievable.
And as I sat-- it's very hard to talk when I think about jail, it was very bad conditions, Kharkiv.
We were in the transport to the concentration camp, and in this transport I found a page of a Russian magazine.
And on this page, I started to learn Russian.
And by the time we arrived in two weeks' time, at the concentration camp, I knew to read and write Russian.
Thank you.
